title: The Realisation Rate Most Queensland Engineering and Architecture Firms Have Never Measured
linkedin:
Most engineering and architecture firms track utilisation. Very few track realisation.

These are not the same number, and the gap between them is often where the year's profit quietly disappears.

When an engineer records eight hours on a project, those eight hours show in the utilisation tally. But the revenue those hours should generate faces a gauntlet before it reaches the bank account. Fixed-fee contracts cap the upside. Scope creep extends the work without extending the fee. Write-offs at project close recover what variation billing missed. Collections run sixty to ninety days behind the work.

The realisation rate captures all of that erosion in one figure: total revenue invoiced and collected, divided by total billable hours at the standard rate. A firm with 10,000 billable hours at $180 per hour has a theoretical capacity revenue of $1.8 million. If it invoiced and collected $1.44 million, the realisation rate is 80 per cent. That 20-point gap is $360,000 in revenue worked for but never captured.

FY26 is now closed, and the data to run this calculation is sitting in your project management system. Government and private commercial work typically realise at meaningfully different rates, and the firm-wide headline often masks a handful of well-run projects carrying the figure while smaller engagements quietly pull it down.

For FY27 planning, this changes the capacity question. It shifts from 'how many people do we need?' to 'what yield do we need from the hours we already have?'

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

If your team is sitting at 75 per cent utilisation, how much of that work actually becomes collected revenue?

Utilisation and realisation are not the same number. The gap between them is where billable hours quietly disappear before they reach the bank account: fixed-fee caps, scope creep, write-offs, and ninety-day collection cycles all take a share before the final tally lands.

FY26 data is sitting in your project management system right now. The new post on the ProfitPulse blog walks through how to calculate your realisation rate and what it changes for your FY27 team plan.

How often does your practice formally review realisation, not just utilisation?

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
google:
If you run an engineering or architecture practice in Brisbane, South East Queensland, or elsewhere on the east coast, the close of FY26 is a natural point to look past the utilisation numbers and ask a sharper question: how much of the time your team spent on projects actually became collected revenue?

Utilisation tells you how busy the team was. Realisation tells you how much of that work translated into invoiced and collected fees. The two figures sit closer together on government infrastructure contracts with structured milestone billing, and further apart on private commercial work where scope moves but fees do not.

With FY26 fully reconciled, engineering and architecture practices now have a complete year of data to calculate their realisation rate for the first time, or to run the calculation properly if it has only been done informally before. The inputs are already in your project management system: billable hours by classification, standard rates, fees invoiced, and fees collected.

For practices planning FY27 capacity, knowing the realisation rate changes the team-sizing conversation from a headcount estimate to a yield-driven plan. A tighter approach to scope documentation, proactive variation billing, and shorter collection cycles can lift realisation meaningfully without any change to headcount.

We have published a new piece on the ProfitPulse blog covering how to calculate the realisation rate from FY26 data, what the project-type breakdown typically reveals, and how to use the number in your FY27 capacity plan. Read the full piece on the blog.
